items.title: Segmental pattern and jump injuries
items.contentHtml:
Jump or segmental lesion

  • Crohn's disease presents segmental involvement with intervening healthy areas (jump lesions), unlike continuous ulcerative colitis.
  • CT/MR enterography shows segments of asymmetrical mural thickening with “cord” sign in phases advanced.
  • Endoscopically: aphthoid ulcers on normal mucosa, which converge into serpiginous ulcers.
  • This pattern is key in the differential diagnosis with infectious colitis (Yersinia, tuberculosis) and Behçet's disease.

items.title: Non-caseating granulomas
items.contentHtml:
Non-caseating granulomas

  • Characteristic (non-pathognomonic) histopathological finding of Crohn's disease: epithelioid granulomas without central necrosis.
  • They are found in 40-60% of surgical biopsies, but only in 15-30% of endoscopic biopsies.
  • They differ from intestinal tuberculosis. (caseating granulomas) and sarcoidosis (non-caseating granulomas but with lung involvement).
  • The presence of granulomas is associated with a higher risk of post-surgical recurrence.

items.title: Transmural involvement
items.contentHtml:
Transmural involvement

  • Inflammation extends to all layers of the intestinal wall (mucosa, submucosa, muscular, serous), unlike ulcerative colitis (limited to mucosa/submucosa).
  • It explains complications: fistulas, abscesses, fibrosis and stenosis.
  • On ultrasound/CT: hypoechoic wall thickening with loss of stratification. mural.
  • The “finger in a glove” sign describes transmural thickening in ileal loops.

items.title: Fistulas and abscesses
items.contentHtml:
Fistulas and abscesses

  • They occur due to transmural involvement that erodes towards neighboring organs (enteroenteric, enterovesical, enterocutaneous, perianal).
  • Perianal fistulas are the initial manifestation in up to 10% of cases.
  • Intra-abdominal abscess requires percutaneous or surgical drainage before starting anti-TNF.
  • Medical treatment of complex perianal fistulas includes infliximab 5 mg/kg in induction (0, 2, 6 weeks) and maintenance every 8 weeks, combined with seton.

items.title: Terminal ileitis
items.contentHtml:
Terminal ileitis

  • Most common involvement in Crohn's disease (70% of cases affect terminal ileum).
  • Clinic: pain in the right iliac fossa, diarrhea, weight loss, fever.
  • Imaging finding: “cord” sign (fibrotic stenosis of the terminal ileum in transit intestinal).
  • Main differential diagnosis: ileocecal tuberculosis (caseating granulomas, culture +, response to tuberculostatics).

items.title: Pain in right iliac fossa
items.contentHtml:
Pain in the right iliac fossa

  • Cardinal symptom of acute terminal ileitis, simulating acute appendicitis (key differential).
  • In Crohn's disease the pain is colicky, intermittent, sometimes with a palpable mass in FID due to an inflammatory plastron.
  • The absence of frank peritoneal signs and the presence of chronic diarrhea provide guidance. to Crohn.
  • Ultrasound: ileal mural thickening >3 mm with haustral loss; associated mesenteric lymphadenopathy.

items.title: Steatorrhea due to malabsorption
items.contentHtml:
Steatorrhea due to malabsorption

  • Occurs due to extensive involvement of the ileum (decreased absorption of bile acids) or due to ileal resection >100 cm.
  • Clinically: fatty, voluminous, malodorous stools, weight loss, deficiency of fat-soluble vitamins (A, D, E, K).
  • Diagnostic test: fecal fat in 72 hours >7 g/day.
  • Treatment includes cholestyramine for biliary diarrhea and vitamin supplements.

items.title: Anti-Saccharomyces cerevisiae antibodies
items.contentHtml:
Anti-Saccharomyces cerevisiae antibodies

  • Serological marker of Crohn's disease (ASCA IgG/IgA), sensitivity 60-70%, specificity 85-90% against ulcerative colitis.
  • ASCA positive + p-ANCA negative suggests Crohn; Negative ASCA + positive p-ANCA suggests ulcerative colitis.
  • They are not used for initial diagnosis, but for doubtful cases or phenotypic classification.
  • ASCA is associated with ileal, penetrating disease and increased risk of surgery.

items.title: Non-curative surgical resection
items.contentHtml:
Non-curative surgical resection

  • Surgery in Crohn's disease is palliative, not curative; Post-surgical recurrence is the rule (50% at 5 years, 70% at 10 years).
  • Surgical indications: symptomatic strictures, uncontrolled fistulas, abscesses, medical failure, dysplasia/cancer.
  • Resection should be as conservative as possible (limited resection) to avoid short bowel syndrome.
  • Postoperative recurrence prophylaxis: metronidazole 20 mg/kg/day for 3 months, and at high risk infliximab or adalimumab.

items.title: Perianal fistula as a manifestation
items.contentHtml:
Perianal fistula as a manifestation

  • Up to 30% of patients with Crohn's disease present perianal disease, which may be the first manifestation.
  • Parks classification: intersphincteric, transsphincteric, suprasphincteric, extrasphincteric.
  • Evaluation with MRI of the pelvis (gold standard) and examination under anesthesia.
  • Treatment: drainage with seton, infliximab 5 mg/kg (induction and maintenance) and in refractory cases advanced surgery (advancement flap).

items.title: Luminal narrowing and stenosis
items.contentHtml:
Luminal narrowing and stenosis

  • Complication due to transmural involvement and fibrosis, which causes partial or complete intestinal obstruction.
  • Differential between inflammatory stenosis (responses to corticosteroids/anti-TNF) and fibrotic stenosis (requires endoscopic dilation or surgery).
  • MR enterography with contrast evaluates mural enhancement (inflammatory activity) vs. absence of enhancement (fibrosis).
  • In symptomatic fibrotic stenosis: endoscopic balloon dilation (up to 18-20 mm) or surgical stricturoplasty.

items.title: Treatment with anti-TNF alpha
items.contentHtml:
Treatment with anti-TNF alfa

  • Indicated in moderate-severe, fistulizing or corticorefractory disease.
  • Infliximab: 5 mg/kg IV in weeks 0, 2, 6, then every 8 weeks. Adalimumab: 160 mg sc week 0, 80 mg week 2, then 40 mg every 2 weeks.
  • Before starting: rule out latent tuberculosis (PPD/IGRA + chest x-ray), HBV, HCV, opportunistic infections.
  • Loss of secondary response can be managed with dose escalation, change to another anti-TNF, or change of mechanism (ustekinumab, vedolizumab).
